name: Raton (SS-270)
events: 9 Aug 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) departed New Orleans, Louisiana for the Panama Canal Zone.
14 Aug 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) arrived at Coco Solo, Panama Canal Zone from New Orleans, Louisiana to begin a training period.
19 Sep 1943 With her trails and initial training completed USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) departed from the Panama Canal zone bound for Brisbane, Australia.
16 Oct 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) arrived at Brisbane.

20 Nov 1943 After moving to Tulagi, Solomon Islands, USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) departed from that base for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bismarck Archipelago / New Guinea area.

26 Nov 1943 While operating north of the Bismarck Archipelago USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) torpedoed and sank the Japanese ammunition ship Onoe Maru (6667 GRT) in position 00°40'N, 148°20'E and torpedoed and sank the Japanese troop transport Kamoi Maru (2811 GRT) in position 00°46'N, 148°20'E.
28 Nov 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) torpedoed and sank the Japanese army cargo ships Hokko Maru (6787 GRT) and Yuri Maru (5346 GRT) north of New Guinea in position 01°40'N, 141°25'E.
6 Dec 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) ended her 1st war patrol at Milne Bay, New Guinea.
11 Dec 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) departed from Milne Bay for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Mindanao / Celebes / Halmahera area.
24 Dec 1943 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) torpedoed and sank the Japanese troop transport Heiwa Maru (5578 GRT) in Kaoe Bay, Halmahera in position 02°57'N, 127°32'E.
2 Jan 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) torpedoed and damaged the Japanese fleet tanker Akebono Maru (10182 GRT) east of Mindanao, Philippines in position 08°18'N, 129°59'E.
25 Jan 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) ended her 2nd war patrol at Fremantle, Australia.
18 Feb 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) departed from Fremantle for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Java and the South China Seas.
14 Apr 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. J.W. Davis) ended her 3th war patrol at Fremantle.
29 Apr 1944 From 29 April to 10 May 1944, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) conducted sound tests, training, fuelled and provisioned. (1)

4 May 1944 Around 0815 H/4, HMAS Adelaide (A/Capt. J.C.D. Esdaile, OBE, RAN) departed Fremantle for exercises with the submarines USS Narwhal (T/Cdr. J.C. Titus, USN), USS Bluefish (T/Cdr. C.M. Henderson, USN) and USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN).
On completion of the exercises HMAS Adelaide set course to make rendezvous with the submarine tender USS Griffin (T/Capt. W.B. Throp, USN).
Rendezvous was affected around 1330 H/6 and they arrived at Fremantle around 2130 H/7. (2)
6 May 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) conducted attack exercises off Fremantle during which USS Chanticleer (T/Lt. P.C. Cottrell, USN) acted as target. (3)
10 May 1944 At 1305 H/10, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) departed from Fremantle for her 4th war patrol. Once again she was ordered to patrol in the Java and South China Seas.
Until 2120 H/10, exercises were carried out with HMAS Dubbo (Lt.Cdr. W.J.C. Webb, RANR).
On completion of these exercises, USS Raton set course for Exmouth Gulf.

11 May 1944 At 0700 H/11, in position 29°42'S, 113°38'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 070° at a range of about 6 miles. This was believed to be HrMs K XV (Lt.Cdr. Baron C.W.T. van Boetzelaer, RNN) which was en-route to Fremantle. Contact was soon lost from sight and it was believed she had submerged.
According to the logbook of HrMs K XV was in position 29°45'S, 113°42'E at 0640 H/11 so this must indeed have been her. (1)

11 May 1944 At 1003 H/11, in position 29°20'S, 113°07'E, the northbound USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted and exchanged recognition signals with the southbound USS Paddle (T/Cdr. B.H. Nowell, USN). (1)
13 May 1944 At 0740 H/13, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) arrived at Exmouth Gulf to top off with fuel. She departed to continue the passage to her patrol area at 0952 H/13. (1)

18 May 1944 At 2150 H/18, off the southern entrance to Lombok Strait, in position 08°52'S, 115°47'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) obtained SJ radar contact bearing 347° at 6500 yards. Course was reversed to avoid as it was thought to be USS Cabrilla (T/Cdr. W.C. Thompson, Jr., USN). She was called twice but there came no reply. However shortly afterwards the contact increased speed and opened out to the eastward. USS Raton meanwhile worked around the contact to the westward and then set course to proceed northwards through Lombok Strait.

USS Cabrilla reported an SJ radar contact at 2158 H/18 bearing 182° at a range of 5750 yards. The target was northbound at 17.5 knots and was thought to be a US submarine so USS Cabrilla pulled clear at full speed to the eastward. (1)

19 May 1944 At 0005 H/19, after having just cleared Lombok Strait, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN), obtained radar contact bearing 302° at a range of 6000 yards. Position was 08°19'S, 115°54'E. USS Raton turned away from the contact, which was obviously a small enemy patrol vessel, and worked around it to the eastward. Range closed to 5700 yards but contact was lost soon afterwards.

At 0035 H/19, in position 08°14'S, 115°59'E, radar contact was obtained bearing 025° at a range of 8000 yards. This was also thought to be a small enemy patrol vessel. USS Raton worked around to the westward. (1)

19 May 1944 At 0410 H/19, in position 07°23'S, 116°13'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 287° at a range of 8200 yards. There was also strong interference on the radar screen and it was considered that this was USS Puffer (T/Cdr. F.G. Selby, USN) which was indeed correct as she too reported contact around this time.
At 0626 H/19, USS Raton sighted a US submarine thought to be USS Puffer bearing 313° at a range of 8000 yards. (1)

23 May 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sank the small Japanese transport vessel Koshin Maru (168 GRT, built ????) with gunfire west of Borneo in position 00°25'S, 107°34'E. Another small merchant vessel was sunk in the same attack.
1408 H/23 - In position 00°27'N, 108°00'E, sighted smoke bearing 086° at a range of about 15 miles. Closed until the masts of two ships were sighted and then worked around to get dead ahead of them.
1512 H/23 - Submerged and commenced approach.

1735 H/23 - Broke off the approach when it was determined that the targets were two small inter-coastal freighters (seatruck-type). The larger one was estimated about 1000-1500 tons and the smaller one 500 to 700 tons. They were considered to be too small to be attacked with torpedoes. It was decided to let them close and then battle surface and sink them with gunfire. Photographs were taken through the periscope. The enemy were not zig-zagging and were on course 265° at a speed of 5 knots. Both had a sail at the forward mast which they later lowered. The larger one had a flat deck, with a mast forward and aft. Both were loaded and the deck load was covered with tarpaulin. Both had a box like superstructure aft and what looked like a twin-mounted machine gun on it was seen on the larger one. The larger also one had a boat hung over its stern. The small one did not had the after mast and also no armament was seen on the aft superstructure. Both ships had a Roman numeral '2' painted on their sail. This apparently, indicated the Japanese Second Expeditionary Force.

1835 H/23 - Battle surfaced in position 00°25'N, 107°34'E and commenced firing at the larger ship. The third shot hit the target in the stern, wrecking the small boat and apparently jamming the targets rudder. The crew, of about 25 men, was seen to abandon ship. The target commenced to circle slowly to the right, smoking heavily. The third hit on the target set her on fire forward. While firing the twelfth shot, the foot firing mechanism on the gun jammed and all firing after that shot had to be done with firing lanyard.

1900 H/23 - The target was now burning fiercely. From explosions and flare-ups, it was believed that her cargo was gasoline in drums. Ceased firing on the first ship after 62 rounds - 12 hits. Closed and strafed her with 20mm. The forward 20mm machine gun jammed after a few rounds but two pans of 20mm were put into the target with the after 20mm machine gin. Closed the second target to 1600 yards and fired 29 rounds - 6 hits. She was smoking but did not catch fire like the first target.

1930 H/23 - The first target was now a mass of flames, with frequent explosions. The second target was sinking rapidly by the stern and when last seen her bow was sticking up about 20 degrees out of the water. Both targets were considered destroyed and course was set to pass to the east of the Tambelan Islands.

24 May 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) torpedoed and sank the Japanese frigate Iki about 220 nautical miles east of Singapore in position 01°17'N, 107°50'E. A hit on one of the transports was also claimed.
Apparently the Japanese frigate Matsuwa also sustains some light damage.

Iki and Matsuwa were escorting convoy HI-63 together with the frigates Kaibokan 9 and Kaibokan 15. The convoy, which was on passage from Manila to Singapore, was made up of the transports Awa Maru (11249 GRT, built 1943), Sanuki Maru (7158 GRT, built 1939), Teia Maru (17537 GRT, built 1932, former French Aramis), Ussuri Maru (6386 GRT, built 1932) and the tankers Kyokuho Maru (10059 GRT, built 1944), Otowasan Maru (9205 GRT, built 1936) and Ryoei Maru (10006 GRT, built 1944). It looks like that not all these ships of the convoy were present when the convoy was attacked. Perhaps the convoy had split up. (All links to Japanese ships are offsite links).

0000 H/24 - Obtained radar contact bearing 101° at a range of 17000 yards. Position was 01°15'N, 108°03'E. It was a dark partially overcast night with poor visibility. Went to battle stations and commenced tracking. The contact developed into a convoy of three large transports and four large destroyer-type escorts. Enemy course was 260° at 13.5 knots. The convoy was not zigzagging. The transports were in column with a destroyer on each bow and quarter of the formation. The largest of the transports was thought to be of at least 10000 tons and the smallest one was thought to be still about 7000-8000 tons. Three of the destroyers were thought to be two-stackers.

0045 H/24 - Heard a very large explosion. This was our first gun target of the previous afternoon blowing up. The glow of the fire, which had still been visible until now, faded out completely. This was about 70 miles to the southwest of our current position.

0102 H/24 - Commenced attack. It was planned to fire six bow torpedoes at the largest and leading transport, then turn and fire four stern torpedoes at the rear destroyer. However, when the range had come down to 4000 yards it was seen that the entire enemy convoy had made a large zig towards and the range closed rapidly. Went ahead flank and turned for a stern tube setup on the nearest destroyer, which was on the starboard bow of the large leading transport.

0116 H/24 - In position 01°17'N, 107°50'E, fired the stern tubes at this destroyer from 3140 yards (torpedo run 2700 yards). 1 minute and 52 seconds after firing the first torpedo hit the destroyer abaft the bridge and it blew up with a tremendous explosion which lit up the whole sky. Debris and flames went up 400 to 500 feet in the air. Apparently the magazine had exploded. Twenty seconds after the first hit another torpedo hit aft but by that time the destroyer had already broken in two and was sinking fast. Fourteen seconds after the second hit a third hit was obtained forward of the bridge on the large, leading, transport which resulted in a large volume of black smoke and some flame. The destroyer went under in less than 2 minutes. It was later considered that the destroyer was actually one of the destroyer escort / frigate type.

Shortly after the destroyer was hit one of the destroyers aft fired two green flares and started to close. Raton pulled out to the north and increased speed to flank. A reload of the after torpedo tubes was immediately commenced. The destroyer was kept astern and the range gradually opened. The enemy occasionally dropped depth charges. The depth of the water was only 25-30 fathoms. The damaged transport was seen to slow down and milling around near the point of the attack together with the other transports and one destroyer. The remaining destroyer had joined up with the one that was giving chase astern of Raton. In about an hour the convoy joined up with them and the gradually worked around to a base course of 290° at 13.5 knots, zigzagging between 260° and 320°. Fire and smoke had ceased on the damaged transport. Apparently the damage inflicted did not slow her down. Course was set to get ahead of the convoy for another attack.

0345 H/24 - Commenced another attack. Planned to fire six bow torpedoes at the leading transport and then turn and fire four stern torpedoes at the rear destroyer. However when the range had come down to 6000 yards the nearest destroyer turned and come straight towards closing the range rapidly. Raton turned and put the enemy astern ready for a 'down the throat shot' at him. However when the range had come down to 4000 yards the enemy turned and rejoined the convoy. Raton set course to regain a position ahead of the convoy for another approach. The transports were still in column but the destroyer appeared to be circling them at a range of 1500 to 2000 yards. Occasionally one of the destroyers would break away and head into the direction of Raton, apparently making a sweep, before turning back to rejoin the formation.

0440 H/24 - Commenced another approach, planning to fire three bow torpedoes at the leading destroyer, three bow torpedoes at the leading transport, then swing and fire four stern torpedoes at the after destroyer.

0446 H/24 - In position 01°44'N, 107°17'E, fired three bow torpedoes at the leading destroyer from 5600 yards following by three bow torpedoes at the leading transport. Swung round and fired four stern torpedoes at a range of 2800 yards at the after destroyer. However it was soon found out that the range for this last shot was 3800 yards and that this had been misunderstood. These torpedoes should not have been fired. All torpedoes fired missed their targets. Raton pulled out to the north-east to reload and then commenced to work around to get ahead of the convoy for yet another attack. The convoy had increased speed to 15 knots and was now on course 305° making for the Anambas Islands about 70 miles away. With only a 3-4 knot speed advantage over the convoy, it would take 5-6 hours for Raton to get ahead off the convoy.
0530 H/24 - It was getting light. Range to the convoy was now 15000 yards and bearing 290°. Broke off the end around and headed for the passage between Great and South Natuna Islands.

27 May 1944 At 0615 H/27, in position 07°46'N, 108°33'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) was shaken up considerably by either two underwater explosions or by striking a submerged object. People in the forward torpedo room thought that something had been struck or that Raton had been strucked by something. T/Cdr. Davis was in the control room at that time, enroute to the bridge, and it appeared to him that two heavy muffled explosions occurred nearby on the port side. Evasive action was taken and course was changed from 305° to 035°. After making the turn a thin oil streak was seen about 1000 yards on the port beam. Nothing further was sighted. Visibility was very good with a flat calm sea.

Later it was determined that USS Raton had been the target of an attack by USS Lapon (T/Cdr. L.T. Stone, USN). Her torpedoes fortunately missed. This is the only instance during the war in which a USN submarine fired torpedoes at another USN submarine. (1)
27 May 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) attacked but missed the German submarine UIT-24 which was on passage from Singapore to Kobe. Position of the attack was 08°39'N, 108°49'E.

1130 H/27 - In position 07°32'N, 108°13'E, sighted smoke bearing 234° at a range of 12 miles. Turned towards to close. The smoke developed in what is thought to be a Japanese Ro-51 class submarine. The enemy was painted light grey and had a small mast forward and aft. Clearing lines stretched from the bridge to the masts, forward and aft. The bridge was low and round. No guns were observed. The enemy was on a base course of 030° at 11 knots. She was zigzagging short legs and was under almost constant helm. The enemy submarine was smoking heavily - brownish black. Raton went to battle stations and started an approach.
1237 H/27 - Secured from battle stations. The closest range obtained was 5000 yards.
1343 H/27 - With the smoke just visible through the periscope at 50 feet, USS Raton surfaced and commenced an end aroun run at full speed on all four main engines.
1818 H/27 - With the target bearing 210° at a range of about 12 miles, Raton submerged and commenced approach for a dusk attack.
1900 H/27 - The target was getting difficult to see. She was not zigzagging.

1934 H/27 - In position 08°39'N, 108°49'E, fired four bow torpedoes from 1850 yards. All missed. All torpedoes had been heard by sound to be running normal but about one minute after firing two of them were heard to stop running.
2000 H/27 - Completed reloading the tubes that had been fired. (1)

28 May 1944 At 0055 H/28, USS Gunnel (T/Cdr. J.S. McCain, Jr., USN) called USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) on TBS. A reply was given and information about the enemy submarine encountered and attacked the day before was passed by USS Raton to USS Gunnel.
At 0541 H/28, USS Raton had not gained contact on the enemy submarine and as dawn was breaking she submerged about 25 miles ahead of the enemy's estimated position and base course.
At 0743 H/28, smoke was sighted bearing 280°. Raton went to a normal approach course and closed at high speed.

At 0747 H/28, the source of the smoke was identified as the same submarine sighted and attacked the day before. Position was 10°18'N, 109°55'E. Raton went to battle stations and continued the approach.
At 0835 H/28, USS Raton secured from battle stations and broke off the approach. The closest range obtained had been 8000 yards.
At 0855 H/28, USS Raton surfaced and commenced an end around run at full speed on all four main engines.

At 1316 H/28, USS Raton was dead ahead of the target which was now on a base course of 030°. Range was about 12 miles. Raton submerged and commenced to close. It was soon seen that the enemy was still zigzagging radically.
At 1422 H/28, the range was now 2500 yards and off the targets track 1200 yards.

At 1427 H/28, all was set to fire. On raising the periscope for a quick look and check of the bearing it was seen that the target had turned and was heading directly towards with a zero angle on the bow at a range of only 500 yards. Raton went to 100 feet. The target passed overhead and close aboard. Undoubtedly the enemy had spotted the periscope.
At 1431 H/28, USS Raton returned to periscope depth. The target was zigzagging wildly. Torpedo fire was held as the chances of hitting were slight.
At 1543 H/28, USS Raton surfaced and commenced another end around at full speed on all four main engines.
At 1925 H/28, the target was bearing 205° at a range of about 12 miles. USS Raton submerged on a normal approach course for a sound-radar approach.

At 2005 H/28, a sound bearing was obtained on the target, 170°. From then the enemy could be tracked on sound as by coming up to 40 feet on several occasions no contact could be gained by using the radar. Sound plot showed the target to be still zigzagging.
At 2055 H/28, the target went by at a range of about 3000 yards still zigzagging radically. The target could not be seen despite the bright moon light night.
At 2112 H/28, Raton surfaced and headed on the target's last bearing at 16 knots.
At 2148 H/28, the smoke of the target was sighted dead ahead at an estimated range of 6000 yards. Raton continued to close. No contact could be obtained with the SJ radar.
At 2208 H/28, the target could barely be made out. Still the enemy could not be picked up on the radar.
At 2215 H/28, the target suddenly disappeared. USS Raton stopped to listen but sound could not pick him up. Undoubtedly Raton had again been sighted.
At 2220 H/28, USS Raton broke off the approach and headed south not willing to take the risk to became the prey instead of the hunter. (1)

29 May 1944 At 1055 H/29, in position 10°32'N, 110°07'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted a US submarine. This submarine was closed and it turned out to be USS Gunnel (T/Cdr. J.S. McCain, Jr., USN). The two submarines subsequently exchanged information before parting company. (1)

31 May 1944 At 1407 H/31, in position 07°00'N, 115°05'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted smoke bearing 080° at a range of about 15 miles. Raton commenced working around to get dead ahead. On closing a stack was seen which appeared to be painted white.

At 1900 H/31, USS Raton was dead ahead of the target at a range of 15 miles. On closing in to gain radar contact it was seen that the target was burning navigation lights. When the range had come down to 5000 yards it was seen to be a properly marked hospital ship which was on course 217° at 13 knots. The silhouette looked like the Yoshino Maru. [This hospital ship was not in the area, we have so far been unable to identify this vessel.] (1)

1 Jun 1944 At 0655 H/1, in position 07°30'N, 116°17'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted smoke bearing 032°. Range was about 20000 yards. Also pinging was heard coming from the same bearing. Raton went to battle stations and commenced an approach to close the range.
At 0705 H/1, the masts of 5 or 6 ships were in sight. Also a twin engined bomber was seen to be patrolling over the convoy.

At 0846 H/1, USS Raton secured from battle stations and broke off the approach. The range could not be closed to less than 18000 yards. The convoy was on course 130° at 10 knots. No escorts could be seen due to the long range but frequent pinging was heard. The convoy was either going to Balabac Strait or it was going to hug the coast of Borneo probably making for Miri.
Course was set to patrol to the north of Brunei Bay but the next day the convoy was not seen and it is thought the convoy had entered the Sulu Sea. (1)

6 Jun 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) attacked a Japanese convoy and torpedoed and sank the Japanese frigate Kaibokan 15 about 160 nautical miles off Cape St. Jacques, French Indo-China in position 08°57'N, 109°17'E.

Kaibokan 15 was part of the escort of convoy HO-02 from Singapore to Moji via Manila (Philippines), Takao and Kirun (both on Formosa). Other escorts (at the time Kaibokan 15 was sunk) were Kaibokan 1, Kaibokan 8 and Kaibokan 20. [All links for the Japanese warships are offsite links and for the composition of the convoy see the website linked to with the Japanese escort vessels.]

1340 H/6 - In position 08°25'N, 108°40'E, sighted smoke bearing 201° at a range of about 20 miles. Commenced tracking. This contact developed into a large convoy of eleven ships with four escorts. Three of the escorts were destroyers and one was believed to be a large patrol vessel or gunboat. Base course of the convoy was 060° at 8 knots.
1348 H/6 - A float plane was sighted over the convoy.
1356 H/6 - A second float plane was sighted over the convoy. One of the planes however soon left and headed west. Apparently it had just been relieved.
1930 H/6 - Went to battle stations and commenced closing submerged to attack.
1940 H/6 - Pinging was picked up on the sound gear.
2120 H/6 - Raton was now dead ahead of the center of the convoy. A setup was made on the leading destroyer.

2123 H/6 - In position 08°58'N, 109°30'E, fired three bow torpedoes at the leading destroyer from only 850 yards. Two hits were obtained and the destroyer blew up with tremendous explosions and broke in two.
2125 H/6 - The stern of the destroyer was seen to go under and soon the destroyer was vertical in the water. She sank rapidly.

2126 H/6 - Five very close and heavy depth charges jarred USS Raton considerably causing some minor damage. Raton immediately went deep. It was later thought these five depth charges were from the sinking destroyer as the other escorts were not close at that moment. While passing 120 feet more depth charges, usually in barrages of seven or eight were exploding as the other escorts were now closing in.

2142 H/6 - Forty-one depth charges had been counted in the last 15 minutes. None were too close but they shook the submarine considerably which had meanwhile gone to 350 feet. Raton gradually withdrew to the westward.
2300 H/6 - The last of the depth charges were heard to explode far away. A total of fifty-seven had now been counted.
2340 H/6 - Raton was now at 65 feet. Smoke was visible through the periscope on bearing 112°.
2351 H/6 - After having surfaced three minutes before, three columns of smoke and one ship were visible. Raton commenced an end around run to get ahead of the convoy.

7 Jun 1944 While making an end around run to gain a position ahead of the convoy already attack the evening before, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) is detected, hunted and depth charged (briefly) by an enemy destroyer.
0029 H/7 - Lost sight of the smoke of the convoy at on bearing 085°. Course was changed to 090° while going ahead full on two of the main engines (possibly charging the batteries with the others).

0137 H/7 - A destroyer was sighted on bearing 000° at a range of about 5 to 6 miles. The stern was put towards the enemy and speed was increased by going to flank ahead on all four main engines. The enemy destroyer was on course 090°.
0150 H/7, the destroyer turned towards apparently having seen the smoke of the submarines engines.

0156 H/7, the destroyer opened fire. A two gun salvo was followed immediately by a 4 gun salvo. It were thought to be either 4" or 5" guns. None of the shots were close. Raton submerged to 65 feet. Went to battle stations and rigged for depth charges. The destroyer could be seen through the periscope, still closing. Course was altered to the westward to keep the enemy on the quarter.
0218 H/7 - The destroyer had closed to about 3000 yards. Angle on the bow was zero and the enemy appeared to be in contact and coming in for an attack. Raton went to 350 feet.
0237 H/7 - The enemy dropped three depth charges, 30 seconds apart. They were not very close. The destroyer continued pinging.
0352 H/7 - Lost contact with the enemy. No pinging or screws could be heard.

0427 H/7 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw the destroyer bearing 135° at a range of about 3000 yards. Sound also had contact on the same bearing and the enemy's screws were heard speeding up and the destroyer was seen to turn towards. Raton went back to 350 feet but no depth charges followed. The destroyer kept pinging and seemed to be right overhead on several occasions but no depth charge attacks were made.

0530 H/7 - Sound reported definitely two and probably three sources of pinging. One was close and the other(s) further away. The close one was very persistent and it was not easy to shake him but eventually it was possible to get away from him.
0700 H/7 - Faint pinging was still heard to the north-east. Came up to 200 feet. Decided not to return to periscope depth until the servicing of the torpedoes had been completed.
0730 H/7 - The last of the pinging was heard to the north-east.
1045 H/7 - Servicing of the torpedoes was completed.
1100 H/7 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. Cdr. Davis decided to remain submerged for the remainder of the day to give all hands a much needed rest. (1)

8 Jun 1944 At 0553 H/8, in position 09°32'N, 110°10'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted a ship bearing 340° at a range of 5 miles. It appeared to be a destroyer. Raton turned away, dived and then turned back to a normal approach course. Course of the enemy was estimated to be around 030° at 15 knots. The enemy could not be seen through the periscope but pinging was heard from his direction.
By 0557 H/8, the pinging grew fainter on bearing 340°. Apparently the enemy had turned away.
At 0740 H/8, USS Raton surfaced and proceeded on course 000°.

At 0905 H/8, the mast and upper structure of a ship was seen through high periscope bearing 045° at a range of about 15 miles. Estimated course of the enemy was 220°. It appeared to be a destroyer. Two minutes later USS Raton submerged and went to a normal approach course closing at 5 knots. USS Raton also went to battle stations.

At 0940 H/8, the enemy had changed course to 180° and was making 15 knots. The closest range obtained was 16000 yards. It was believed to be the same ship that had been sighted at 0553 H/8. It was not possible to positively identify the type of ship. It looked the most like an old type destroyer but it had only one mast and its stack was abnormally high and thinner than a destroyers. It smoked quite frequently. It was thought possible to be a Q-ship.
At 1040 H/8, USS Raton was abaft the targets beam and the range was opening. Secured from battle stations.
At 1105 H/8, USS Raton surfaced. There was nothing in sight. Course was set to 330°. (1)

9 Jun 1944 At 1503 H/9, in position 08°46'N, 108°41'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted USS Gunnel (T/Cdr. J.S. McCain, Jr., USN) which was closed. Information was exchanged and arrangements were made to make rendezvous again tomorrow [this did not take place though]. (1)
10 Jun 1944 At 1300 H/10, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) left her current patrol area to proceed to patrol in the Java Sea. (1)
13 Jun 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sank a sailing vessel (Chinese type junk named Kheng Seng Lie) by holing it in the Java Sea in approximate position 04°12'S, 109°46'E.
1705 H/13 - In position 04°12'S, 109°46'E, sighted a large sailing vessel through the periscope bearing 108°. The vessel was on a north-easterly course. Commenced closing.

1842 H/13 - Surfaced and closed the sailing vessel on four engine speed. It was seen to be a large three-mast Chinese type junk. Went close aboard and fired a burst of .30cal across its bow, but it did not heave to. Shouted to them to douse sails, but the crew either ignored it or did not understand. Thried cutting the sails down with .30cal gunfire but this did not succeed. It was flying a white flag with a red star in the center over a Japanese merchant flag. The bow was put alongside and the sailing vessel was held with two lines and grapnels. One of the crew of the sailing vessel handed over the ship's papers. Meanwhile the whole crew was ordered on board the submarine. There were eleven in total, all Chinese. A party boarded the sailing vessel to lower the sails and inspect the ship and its cargo. The vessel, named Kheng Seng Lie was loaded. It had the number D 5607 painted in white on the deck house. The cargo consisted of 360 bags of granulated sugar and 100 bags of raw sugar. According to the crew they were en-route to Singapore. They also said the vessel operated regularly between Singapore and Surabaya with stops en-route. They were originally from Hainan Island. The crew was sent below and all were given a bath, physical exams and dry clothes. All were fairly clean and no diseases were discovered among them. They were all frightened though but by the next day they had loosened up considerably. They were given cigarettes and plenty to eat. Two of them spoke a little English.

2000 H/13 - The junk saink quickly, bows first without a warning. Apparently the pounding had knocked a hole in her hull which had not been noticed. The heavy cargo probably made her sink like a rock. The boarding party of two officers and three men did not had the time to evacuate and ended up in the water but fortunately all were recovered in a short time. Three of them had very narrow escapes when the junk suddenly sank.
2035 H/13 - Set course for Bawean Island, clearing the area. (1)

17 Jun 1944 USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) torpedoed and sank the small Japanese transport Akegata Maru [name according to the survivors] (???? GRT, built ????) in the Java Sea in position 05°48'N, 113°51'E.

0744 H/17 - While patrolling submerged in position 05°52'S, 113°45'E, sighted the masts of a ship bearing 252° at a range of about 8 miles. The vessel was proceeding on an east-north-easterly course. The vessel was lost from sight almost immediately in a heavy rain squall. Course was set to close to investigate.

0830 H/17 - The ship was now in sight. Went to battle stations and continued the approach. Determined the ship to be a small intercoastal freighter of 2000-2500 tons. She had two masts, was diesel powered and had a small low stack aft. A deck load was seen to be covered with tarpaulin. A sail was up on the forward mast. The ship was not zigzagging and was proceeding at 4 knots on course 070°.

0959 H/17 - In position 05°48'S, 113°51'E, fired three torpedoes from 850 yards. The third torpedo hit the vessel aft which quickly sank, in about two minutes, stern first. Several survivors were seen in the water as was a considerable amount of wreckage.

1020 H/17 - Surfaced and closed the wreckage. It had been a wooden ship and large pieces of it were still floating. One Japanese was picked up, an engineer officer. Also eight natives from Celebes were picked up. Some papers were also picked up. All survivors were sent below, given bathes, physical exams, dry clothes, cigarettes and food. None had any disceases or were injured except for some minor scratches. Two of them could speak a little English. They said the ship was named Akegata Maru of about 2500 tons and was en-route from Surabaya to Banjermasin, Borneo. It had a cargo of sugar, rice, salt and four Japanese automobiles. The crew had been 31 in number, 6 Japanese officers and 25 natives. When the natives went down below they kept pointing at the Japanese survivor saying 'Nippon'.
1130 H/17 - Cleared the area and set course for Lombok Strait to leave patrol and return to Fremantle. (1)

18 Jun 1944 At 0900 H/18, just north of the northern entrance to Lombok Strait, in position 08°09'S, 115°48'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted a two-masted sailing vessel bearing 252° at a range of about 4 miles. Course of the vessel was 110°. Pictures were taken but due to the proximity of Lombok Strait the vessel was not attacked.
Around 1200 H/18, USS Raton commenced the, submerged, southbound passage of Lombok Strait.
At 1905 H/18, USS Raton surfaced, still not having cleared the Strait.
Around 2000 H/18, USS Raton cleared Lombok Strait. No enemy patrols had been encountered. (1)

22 Jun 1944 At 1610 H/22, in position 29°26'S, 113°11'E, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) sighted USS Rasher (T/Cdr. W.R. Laughton, USN) bearing 011° at a range of 8 miles. Recognition signals were exchanged and the submarines closed each other. Two packages of papers were transferred by line throwing gun from USS Raton to USS Rasher which was also bound for Fremantle. (1)
23 Jun 1944 Around 0850 H/23, USS Raton (T/Cdr. J.W. Davis, USN) ended her 4th war patrol at Fremantle.
At Fremantle she was taken in hand for refit by USS Orion (T/Capt. C.S. Isgrig, USN).
18 Jul 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) departed from Fremantle for her 5th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea.
10 Sep 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) ended her 5th war patrol at Fremantle.
6 Oct 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) departed from Fremantle for her 6th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea.

18 Oct 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and sank the Japanese army cargo ships Taikai Maru (3812 GRT) and Shiranesan Maru (4739 GRT) in the South China Sea, south-west of Luzon, Philippines in position 12°37'N, 118°46'E.
27 Oct 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) departed from Mios Woendi, Schouten Islands, after she took onboard additional torpedoes and fuel, to resume her 6th war patrol.
1 Nov 1944 At 0013 H/1, in position 09°54'N, 121°33'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) exchanged recognition signals with the SJ radar with USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea, USN). (4)
6 Nov 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and damaged the Japanese heavy cruiser Kumano (offsite link) west of Lingayen, Philippines in position 16°11'N, 119°44'E.

14 Nov 1944 While operating north-north-west of Cape Bolinao, Luzon, Philippines USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and damaged the Japanese supply ship Kurasaki (2371 GRT, offsite link) in position 17°41'N, 118°00'E and torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant tanker Unkai Maru No.5 (2841 GRT) in position 17°48'N, 117°58'E.
The Kurasaki sank the following day north-northwest of Cape Bolinao in position 17°27'N, 117°43'E as a result of the damage inflicted by USS Raton.
1 Dec 1944 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea) ended her 6th war patrol at San Francisco. She is now sent to the Mare Island Navy Yard for a major overhaul.
13 Mar 1945 With her overhaul completed USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) departed from the US west coast bound for Pearl Harbor.
20 Apr 1945 USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) departed from Pearl Harbor for her 7th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Yellow Sea.

24 Apr 1945 At 0037 I/24, in position 25°00'N, 137°20'E, USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. M.W. Shea, USN) obtained SJ radar contact bearing 010° at a range of 6000 yards. Avoiding action was taken. It was thought to be a Guam bound US submarine.

At 0105 I/24, USS Seahorse (T/Cdr. H.H. Greer, Jr., USN) reported picking up radar interference of USS Raton on an opposite course. Calls were exchanged with the SJ radar. So most likely the contact picked up by USS Raton was USS Seahorse. (5)
2 May 1945 USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant cargo ship Toryu Maru (1992 GRT) in the Yellow Sea southeast of the Shantung Peninsula in position 37°24'N, 123°50'E.
12 May 1945 USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant cargo ship Rekizan Maru (1311 GRT) in the Yellow Sea off the Shantung Peninsula in position 37°25'N, 123°42'E.
16 May 1945 USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) torpedoed and sank the Japanese merchant cargo ship Eiju Maru (2456 GRT) in the Yellow Sea off the west coast of Korea in position 37°34'N, 124°13'E.
25 May 1945 USS Raton (Cdr. M.W. Shea) ended her 7th war patrol at Guam.
22 Jun 1945 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta) departed from Guam for her 8th war patrol. She was ordered to patrol off Hong Kong.
23 Jul 1945 USS Raton (Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta) ended her 8th war patrol at Subic Bay, Philippines.

31 Aug 1945 The submarine tender USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Blenny (T/Lt.Cdr. C.H. Fuller, Jr., USN), USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN), USS Boarfish (T/Lt.Cdr. E.C. Blonts, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) departed Subic Bay. All were to proceed to Pearl Harbour except USS Blenny, USS Blueback and USS Boarfish which were to proceed to Guam.

14 Sep 1945 The submarine tender USS USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Subic Bay.
